name: Shipwright
summary: A builder who constructs and repairs wooden or metal vessels—among the most consequential trades in maritime history from Viking longships to naval frigates.
duties: Loft hull lines, frame timbers or weld plates, caulk seams, fit rigging attachments, and survey damage for repair.
skills: Structural geometry, materials knowledge, teamwork in yards, safety around heavy lifts.
tools: Adzes historically, rivet guns, cranes, lofting floors, CAD in modern yards.
regions: Venice Arsenal, Baltic oak yards, Plymouth dockyards, modern Asian mega-yards.
originLabel: Early plank-built seafaring hulls
educationPath: Shipyard apprenticeships, naval architecture adjacent training, welding certifications.
modernSectors: Naval shipyards, yacht builders, repair docks, offshore fabrication.

events.label: Industrial iron hulls
events.description: Steam and iron transform ship construction from artisan wood shops to capital-intensive yards.

name: Boat Builder
summary: A craft worker who builds smaller vessels—fishing boats, dinghies, and launches—often in family yards distinct from warship slipways.
duties: Shape hulls, install engines or oarlocks, fiberglass or plank construction, and sea-trial fittings.
skills: Hands-on fabrication, client consultation, weight balance, small-business operations.
tools: Molds, planes, epoxy systems, outboard test tanks, trailer fittings.
regions: Coastal villages, Maine lobster boat shops, Mediterranean fishing ports.
originLabel: River and coastal small-craft traditions
educationPath: Apprenticeship in yards, marine trade schools, composite certification courses.
modernSectors: Custom yacht tenders, workboats, rowing shells, rental fleet maintenance.

events.label: Fiberglass revolution
events.description: GRP hulls democratize production beyond plank-on-frame shops.

name: Rigger
summary: A specialist who installs and maintains a ship's masts, ropes, and sails—or modern crane and stage rigging with overlapping skills.
duties: Splice rope, set up spars, tune standing rigging, load cargo slings, and inspect wear.
skills: Knotcraft, height comfort, load calculation, maintenance scheduling, teamwork aloft.
tools: Marlin spikes, shackles, winches, climbing harnesses, crane signals.
regions: Age-of-sail navies and merchant fleets; modern port cargo and entertainment rigging.
originLabel: Rope-spar systems on early sailing ships

events.label: Steam reduces sail crews
events.description: Merchant sail fleets shrink though yacht rigging keeps skills alive.

name: Longshoreman
summary: A dockworker who loads and unloads cargo ships—brutal hand-labor historically, now intertwined with container cranes and union halls.
duties: Operate gear, stow containers, secure lashings, drive yard tractors, and follow safety rules in terminals.
skills: Crane signaling, stamina, union dispatch literacy, hazard awareness, teamwork.
tools: Twistlocks, spreaders, chassis, RFID gate systems, steel-toed boots.
regions: Global container ports; ILA on US East Coast, ILWU on West Coast historically pivotal.
originLabel: Manual stevedoring at industrial docks

events.label: Containerization
events.description: Standard boxes reshape dock labor and global trade geography.

name: Stevedore
summary: A firm or foreman class that manages cargo stowage in ships' holds—coordinating gangs of longshoremen for efficient port turnaround.
duties: Plan stowage, direct gangs, liaise with ship officers, and ensure cargo integrity and safety.
skills: Load planning, leadership, language of cargo marks, union negotiation, time pressure management.
tools: Stowage plans, cargo manifests, radios, tally sheets, terminal operating systems.
regions: Major entrepôt ports: Singapore, Rotterdam, Los Angeles/Long Beach.
originLabel: Steamship-era discharge contractors

events.label: Bulk and breakbulk specialization
events.description: Oil, grain, and general cargo terminals employ specialized stevedoring firms.

name: Harbor Pilot
summary: A licensed navigator who boards ships to guide them through dangerous inlets and harbors—combining local knowledge with liability for the largest vessels afloat.
duties: Embark by ladder or helicopter, conn vessels through channels, advise masters on tides and traffic, and hand back at sea.
skills: Shiphandling, bridge communication, tidal calculation, calm authority, night pilotage.
tools: Pilot boats, portable pilot units, radar overlays, VTS radio, climbing gear.
regions: Every major choke point: Panama approaches, Suez, Mississippi delta, English Channel.
originLabel: Licensed pilots in European port guilds
educationPath: Maritime academy, ship command experience, pilot apprenticeship and licensing exams.
modernSectors: State pilot associations, compulsory pilotage districts, LNG mega-ship moves.

events.label: Exxon Valdez aftermath
events.description: Environmental liability raises pilotage scrutiny in sensitive waterways worldwide.

name: Ship Navigator
summary: An officer who determines position and plans ocean passages—evolving from astrolabe users to GPS watch officers on bridge teams.
duties: Plot courses, maintain charts, compute celestial fixes historically, monitor electronics, and comply with COLREGS.
skills: Mathematics, attention on watch, weather routing, collision avoidance rules, log discipline.
tools: Sextants historically, ECDIS, radar ARPA, almanacs, passage planning software.
regions: Merchant marine globally; navigation officers on every blue-water trade route.
originLabel: Phoenician and Greek celestial wayfinding
educationPath: Maritime academy third officer pathway, STCW certifications, celestial competency exams.
modernSectors: Container lines, tankers, cruise ships, research vessels.

events.label: GPS era approaches
events.description: Satellite navigation gradually supplants celestial fixes for routine watchkeeping.

name: Merchant Mariner
summary: A licensed seafarer on commercial vessels distinct from naval crews—carrying world trade under flags of convenience and national registries.
duties: Stand watches, maintain ship systems, handle cargo operations, and survive long contracts away from home.
skills: STCW competencies, teamwork, isolation tolerance, safety drills, multilingual crews.
tools: Immersion suits, GMDSS radios, engine room logs, mooring stations.
regions: Global shipping lanes; Philippines, Ukraine, India major crew supply nations.
originLabel: Hanseatic and Mediterranean merchant crews
educationPath: Maritime academies, rating pathways, endorsement upgrades.
modernSectors: Bulk carriers, tankers, containers, offshore supply vessels.

events.label: Safety conventions
events.description: Titanic disaster spurs international merchant safety rule development.

name: Deckhand
summary: A junior sailor who handles lines, paints, and cargo on deck—the entry rung of seagoing careers on fishing boats and merchant ships alike.
duties: Mooring, chipping rust, assist cargo, stand lookout, and clean spaces under bosun direction.
skills: Knots, physical stamina, prompt obedience, basic maintenance, watchkeeping support.
tools: Paint brushes, winches, hoses, PFDs, hand tools.
regions: Coastal fisheries and global merchant fleets.
originLabel: General deck labor on early traders
educationPath: Sea time accumulation, rating certifications, maritime training institutes.
modernSectors: Fishing vessels, tugs, ferries, offshore wind support boats.

events.label: Mechanization on deck
events.description: Hydraulic winches and self-unloaders change but do not end deckhand roles.

name: Boatswain
summary: The senior deck petty officer who leads maintenance and cargo work—a title unchanged from Royal Navy tradition to modern merchant marine.
duties: Supervise deck crew, maintain rigging and deck gear, run maintenance schedules, and train juniors.
skills: Leadership, rigging knowledge, safety enforcement, conflict management aboard cramped ships.
tools: Bosun's locker inventories, power tools, cargo securing gear, work lists.
regions: Naval and merchant vessels worldwide; bosun pipes still symbolize the role.
originLabel: Deck foremen on medieval cog and galley crews

events.label: Navy rating codification
events.description: Royal Navy formalizes petty officer structure including boatswain mates.

name: Sea Captain
summary: The master of a merchant vessel with ultimate command authority at sea—legally responsible for ship, cargo, and crew from sail era to modern bridge.
duties: Voyage planning, commercial liaison with owners, safety management, discipline, and emergency command.
skills: Navigation, leadership, maritime law literacy, engineering overview, crisis judgment.
tools: Bridge consoles, SMS manuals, charter parties, GMDSS distress kits.
regions: Every ocean trade; captains hold licenses issued by flag states.
originLabel: Merchant ship masters in Bronze Age trade
educationPath: Officer license progression to master mariner unlimited, sea time, exams.
modernSectors: Container, tanker, cruise, offshore, yacht charter captains.

events.label: Titanic inquiry
events.description: Disaster inquiries reinforce master responsibility doctrines in international law.

name: First Mate
summary: The second-in-command on a vessel who relieves the captain, oversees cargo, and leads deck officers—title also chief mate on merchant ships.
duties: Cargo planning, watch supervision, safety officer tasks, crew discipline, and assuming command when needed.
skills: Organizational leadership, stability calculations, personnel management, regulatory audits.
tools: Cargo plans, muster lists, ISM checklists, bridge watch schedules.
regions: Merchant and fishing fleets; chief officer rank on STCW licenses.
originLabel: Structured officer hierarchies on European merchants

events.label: STCW standards
events.description: International convention standardizes officer competencies including chief mate duties.

name: Whaler
summary: A crew member on ships that hunted whales for oil and baleen—economically vital in the 19th century and morally condemned into extinction as an industry.
duties: Track whales, man whaleboats, harpoon strikes, tow carcasses, render blubber on try-pots or factory ships.
skills: Courage, small-boat seamanship, endurance, flensing knife work, seasonal migration knowledge.
tools: Harpoon guns, lances, try-pots, factory flensing decks.
regions: North Atlantic, South Pacific, Arctic; New Bedford and Nantucket historic hubs.
originLabel: Open-boat pelagic whaling expansion

events.label: Petroleum rises
events.description: Kerosene reduces economic dependence on whale oil lighting.

events.label: Commercial moratorium
events.description: IWC moratorium ends most commercial whaling fleets.

name: Ship Chandler
summary: A supplier who provisions vessels with food, rope, paint, and spare parts—indispensable between voyages in every port district.
duties: Stock warehouses, fill slop chest orders, deliver alongside ships, and manage credit with ship masters.
skills: Inventory breadth, urgent logistics, multilingual orders, credit risk judgment.
tools: Warehouse bins, delivery boats, ERP systems, bond stores for tax-free supply.
regions: Port warehouse districts worldwide; chandlers row traditions in old harbors.
originLabel: Colonial port supply houses

events.label: Steamship provisioning scale
events.description: Larger crews and coal bunkering expand chandlery product lines.

name: Marine Engineer
summary: An officer who operates and maintains ship propulsion and auxiliary machinery—from coal stokers to modern LNG dual-fuel plants.
duties: Watch engine rooms, troubleshoot plants, oversee bunkering, maintain logs, and lead repair during port calls.
skills: Thermodynamics, diesel and turbine literacy, safety culture, watch teamwork, pollution compliance.
tools: Engine control rooms, oily water separators, diagnostic software, PPE in hot spaces.
regions: Merchant fleets and naval engineering branches globally.
originLabel: Steam engine rooms on early steamers
educationPath: Marine engineering degrees, EOOW certification, sea time as cadet.
modernSectors: Container ships, cruise liners, offshore platforms, icebreakers.

events.label: Diesel dominance
events.description: Internal combustion and later slow-speed diesels define engineer officer careers.

name: Naval Architect
summary: A designer who engineers hull forms and stability for ships—merging physics, materials, and regulations for safe floating structures.
duties: Model resistance, specify scantlings, oversee model tests, approve construction drawings, and sign stability books.
skills: Hydrodynamics, structural analysis, classification society rules, CAD, project management.
tools: CFD software, towing tanks, FEA, class society rule engines.
regions: Classification societies, navy design bureaus, yacht design studios.
originLabel: Systematic ship theory in Enlightenment Europe
educationPath: Naval architecture degrees, class society graduate programs.
modernSectors: Commercial yards, defense design, offshore structures, superyacht studios.

events.label: Ironclad design races
events.description: Armored warships accelerate formal naval architecture education.

name: Sailmaker
summary: A craft worker who cuts and stitches sails for vessels—vital in age-of-sail navies and still present in racing yacht lofts.
duties: Measure rigs, cut panels, sew seams, repair blowouts, and advise on sail inventories.
skills: Textile knowledge, sewing precision, load path intuition, customer measurement visits.
tools: Sewing palms, heavy machines, sailcloth rolls, spreader bars for loft floors.
regions: Port sail lofts historically; modern high-tech membrane sailmakers in New Zealand and France.
originLabel: Woven sailcloth on ancient Mediterranean craft

events.label: Steam displaces sail commerce
events.description: Merchant sailmakers shrink though yacht racing sustains lofts.

name: Ropemaker
summary: A manufacturer of cordage for rigging, anchors, and docks—central to seaports until synthetic fibers and steel wire replaced hemp at scale.
duties: Spin yarns, lay ropes on long walks, treat tar, and supply chandlers with standardized lengths.
skills: Fiber tension judgment, length measurement, tar application, strength testing.
tools: Rope walks, spinning wheels, tarring vats, measuring reels.
regions: Dockside ropewalks in Boston, Plymouth, and Baltic ports through 19th century.
originLabel: Guild ropemakers serving port cities

events.label: Nylon synthetics
events.description: Synthetic lines replace hemp and manila in most marine applications.

name: Fishmonger
summary: A retail trader who sells fish and shellfish—bridging fishermen and urban consumers in markets from ancient agoras to supermarket seafood counters.
duties: Source catch, ice displays, fillet to order, advise customers, and manage food safety temperatures.
skills: Freshness judgment, knife work, customer service, early morning sourcing, inventory rotation.
tools: Display ice, fillet knives, scales, refrigerated cases.
regions: Coastal cities worldwide; Billingsgate and Tsukiji traditions famous.
originLabel: Urban fish markets in classical ports
educationPath: Market apprenticeships, food safety certification, wholesale buying relationships.
modernSectors: Street markets, supermarket seafood departments, restaurant supply.

events.label: Refrigerated rail
events.description: Inland cities gain daily fishmonger trade beyond coastal catch.

name: Customs Officer
summary: A border official who inspects cargo and collects duties at ports and airports—enforcing tariff law since ancient transit tolls.
duties: Examine manifests, search contraband, classify goods, assess duties, and facilitate trusted trader programs.
skills: Regulatory knowledge, inspection technique, integrity, language skills, risk targeting.
tools: X-ray scanners, drug detection kits, tariff databases, seal verification tools.
regions: Customs houses at every major port; WCO harmonized system globally.
originLabel: Transit duties in ancient empires
educationPath: Customs academies, law enforcement crossover, trade compliance courses.
modernSectors: Port of entry inspection, brokerage liaison, anti-smuggling units.

events.label: GATT trade rounds
events.description: Postwar trade talks reshape tariff administration and customs modernization.

name: Marine Surveyor
summary: An independent inspector who assesses hull condition, damage, and cargo for insurers and buyers—impartial expertise in casualty and pre-purchase surveys.
duties: Inspect plating, document damage, attend loading surveys, write reports for underwriters, and testify in disputes.
skills: Corrosion recognition, report writing, classification rule literacy, negotiation neutrality.
tools: Thickness gauges, moisture meters, drones, survey report templates.
regions: Lloyd's surveyor traditions; global P&I club networks.
originLabel: Hull insurance survey practices in Lloyd's coffee house era
educationPath: Naval architecture or marine experience, surveyor company training, class society pathways.
modernSectors: P&I clubs, hull insurers, ship sale due diligence, casualty investigation.

events.label: Lloyd's Register formalization
events.description: Classification and survey institutions standardize hull inspection professions.

name: Tugboat Captain
summary: A master of powerful small vessels that push and pull ships in harbors—precision shiphandling at slow speeds with enormous bollard pull.
duties: Connect tow lines, maneuver assisted ships through locks and berths, coordinate VTS, and maintain tug readiness.
skills: Shiphandling finesse, communication with pilots, engine knowledge, weather judgment in tight quarters.
tools: Winches, z-drives, VHF, fendering, tow wires and synthetic hawsers.
regions: Every major harbor; iconic competitions among skilled tug masters.
originLabel: Steam tugs assisting sail and early steamers
educationPath: Tug company license progression, pilotage-adjacent experience, local knowledge apprenticeships.
modernSectors: Harbor towage, offshore rig moves, salvage support, icebreaking assists.

events.label: Exxon Valdez tow response
events.description: Salvage and tug operations gain public visibility in environmental disasters.

name: Canal Lock Keeper
summary: An operator who opens gates and valves to raise or lower boats through canal locks—once essential on inland waterways before automated controls.
duties: Operate paddles, guide boats, maintain weed gates, sell passage tickets historically, and keep traffic logs.
skills: Hydraulic intuition, customer patience, maintenance of wooden gates, seasonal ice management.
tools: Windlass handles, gate keys, VHF, weed rakes, ticket books.
regions: Panama Canal locks, Erie Canal heritage, European narrowboat networks.
originLabel: Canal age lock staffing in Britain and France

events.label: Panama Canal opens
events.description: Massive lock complexes employ specialized lock operating crews.

name: Submarine Officer
summary: A naval officer who commands or departments on submersible warships—elite community demanding engineering literacy and psychological stamina under confinement.
duties: Drive ship, manage torpedo or missile departments, conduct dives, maintain nuclear propulsion watch where applicable.
skills: Leadership, sonar interpretation, classified discipline, reactor or diesel plant knowledge, stress tolerance.
tools: Periscopes historically, sonar stacks, fire control, reactor controls on SSBN/SSN classes.
regions: US, UK, France, Russia, China, and other submarine-operating navies.
originLabel: Royal Navy Holland-class operational submarines
educationPath: Naval academy, submarine school, qualifying dives, department head pipelines.
modernSectors: Attack submarines, ballistic missile patrols, research subs, private submersible tourism fringe.

events.label: USS Nautilus
events.description: Nuclear propulsion extends submerged endurance and transforms submarine officer careers.
